name: CONTRACT-BACKLOG-36
title: Architectural review: feature-size budget + split criteria (when should a big feature become multiple features?)
description:
Architectural concern raised during prompt-size analysis: should feature spec size have a budget? At what threshold should we split a feature?

Concrete trigger: rabbit-cage spec.md is ~80KB after wave-7 cleanup. It has 7 'Invariants' sections covering wildly different concerns (Claude Code surface deployment, hooks runtime, scope-guard, rabbit-config skill, scripts, runtime-flag display, output schemas, etc.). Each section could plausibly be its own feature.

Two related questions:

1. SIZE BUDGET — should features have a maximum spec size (in lines, bytes, or invariant count)? Three plausible approaches:
   (a) HARD limit — feature MUST split when over threshold. Forces architectural review but can produce awkward splits.
   (b) SOFT signal — WARNING threshold (e.g., 50KB or 30+ invariants) prompts 'consider whether this feature should be split'. HARD threshold (e.g., 100KB or 60+ invariants) requires a 'no-split rationale' justification invariant.
   (c) NO limit — size is what it is; split decisions are purely semantic.

2. SPLIT CRITERIA — what signals indicate a feature should be split? Observed in rabbit-cage:
   - Multiple 'Invariants' sections in one spec (rabbit-cage has 7)
   - Invariant count above some threshold (rabbit-cage post-renumber: ~90+ invariants)
   - Invariants cluster by topic (surface, hooks, scope-guard, skill, etc. are distinct concerns)
   - Different consumers dispatch different subsets (e.g., session-init.py only cares about hooks invariants; not skill invariants)
   - Cycles touch different sections without touching others
   - A 'sub-skill' or sub-tool inside a feature feels like its own thing (e.g., /rabbit-config skill inside rabbit-cage)

Counterargument: splits aren't free.
- More cross-feature contracts to maintain
- More cross-feature ripples (we saw plenty: ws35 rabbit-bug/rabbit-backlog cleanup, ws36 KNOWN_ISSUES management, ws39 wave-7 spec drift)
- More files per feature (spec.md, contract.md, run.py, etc.)
- Higher discovery cost ('where does X live?')

Specific concrete proposal to evaluate:
Consider splitting rabbit-cage into:
- rabbit-cage (Claude Code surface deployment — build.py, copy-file management, .claude/skills/, .claude/hooks/ population)
- rabbit-hooks (runtime hook scripts — scope-guard, sync-check, session-init, refresh, _runtime_flags)
- rabbit-config (the /rabbit-config skill + its scripts)
- rabbit-project (rabbit-project*, workspace-tree)

Each would be ~15-25KB spec. Total cross-feature contract surface increases significantly.

Acceptance:
- Decide soft-limit thresholds (or that none should exist)
- Decide signal-based split criteria
- Apply to rabbit-cage specifically: confirm split or confirm stay-monolithic with documented rationale
- If split: file follow-up backlogs for each split. This backlog is the decision, not the execution.
- Update contract spec to add 'feature size discipline' invariants if size-budget approach is chosen
- Companion to CONTRACT-BACKLOG-35 (prompt-scoping optimization): both reduce per-cycle cost; splits AND scoped prompts are complementary not redundant.

This is design work, deserves brainstorming session up front.

history.note: Closed in CONTRACT-WAVE-9: empirical answer arrived at via 9 housekeeping waves — NO hard feature-size budget; trigger audit waves when bug density signals spec drift. The meta-contract architecture (Plans A-F) that BACKLOG-36 originally drove is now in place; this audit wave is the final per-feature audit before Plan E.* per-feature meta-contract migrations. References: docs/2026-05-23-housekeeping-protocol.md (wave sequence + audit criteria) and docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-23-meta-contract-architecture-design.md (architecture design).
